results.title: MarginTradingHook#updateOrder lacks access control
results.reasoning: Yes. Item 1 of the audit explicitly identifies that MarginTradingHook.updateOrder lacks the ownership check (order.initPosId == initPosId), allowing any user to modify any active order. It references the same function and code path as the description, contrasts it with cancelOrder’s check, and implies the same fix. Fixing the missing initPosId equality check would resolve both issues, so they are the same vulnerability.

results.title: Order’s creator can update tokenOut to arbitrary token
results.reasoning: The reported issue is that updateOrder fails to validate tokenOut remains one of the position’s base/quote assets, allowing the order creator to change tokenOut to an arbitrary token. The proposed fix is to enforce _tokenOut == baseAsset || _tokenOut == quoteAsset in updateOrder. The audit’s closest item (#1) flags missing authorization in updateOrder (any user can modify any order), which is a different root cause and requires a different fix (ownership check). Fixing the audit’s issue would not prevent the order owner from setting tokenOut to an arbitrary token. No other audit item mentions tokenOut validation in updateOrder. Therefore, the specific vulnerability was not detected.

results.title: fillOrder executor can be front-run by the order creator by changing order’s limitPrice_e36, the executor’s assets can be stolen
results.reasoning: The report does not identify the specific front‑running/slippage manipulation where the order creator updates limitPrice_e36 just before fillOrder executes to extract value from the executor. Issue #1 discusses missing authorization in updateOrder allowing any user to modify any order’s parameters (including limitPrice), which is an access control flaw affecting victims’ orders, not the creator’s ability to front‑run their own order fill. Fixing #1 (adding ownership checks) would not prevent the H‑03 attack, since the creator would still be authorized to change limitPrice. No other listed findings mention bounding/fixing limitPrice at fill time or validating it against executor‑provided constraints. Therefore, the specific vulnerability was not detected.
